title: Agent Patterns & Production Intelligence
subtitle: ReAct reasoning, multi-agent architectures, and the patterns that separate demos from production systems
heroTitle: Agent Patterns & Production Intelligence

objectives.title: Master the ReAct Pattern
objectives.description: Understand Reason + Act: how production agents think before they act, observe results, and adapt. Build an agent that reasons visibly through a multi-step research task.

objectives.title: Understand Multi-Agent Architectures
objectives.description: Learn three patterns вҖ” Pipeline, Debate, and Orchestrator вҖ” and when each is the right call. Design a multi-agent system on paper.

objectives.title: Build a ReAct Research Agent
objectives.description: Hands-on: take your Session 5 tools (Wikipedia, web fetch, calculator) and add a ReAct system prompt. Same tools, same loop вҖ” one paragraph transforms the behavior. Then push it to failure with real APIs.

objectives.title: Evaluate Agent ROI for Business
objectives.description: Apply the Automation Matrix to decide what to automate, what to augment, and what stays human. Calculate agent ROI with a real framework.

agenda.title: Part 1: The Pattern Gap
agenda.description: Bridge from Session 5's NanoClaw demo вҖ” the patterns behind production agents.
bulletPoints: When NanoClaw researched for us live, did it call one tool and stop? No. It searched, evaluated, searched again, then answered. That's a PATTERN.
The Competence Ladder: Tool Use (Session 4) вҶ’ Real APIs + Memory (Session 5) вҶ’ ReAct + Multi-Agent + MCP (TODAY)
Three patterns we'll master: ReAct (think before acting), Multi-Agent (team of specialists), MCP (universal tool standard)
ACTION: Open Colab notebook now. API key ready. We're building a research agent that thinks before it acts.

agenda.title: Part 2: ReAct Deep Dive
agenda.description: The pattern that powers every serious AI agent: Reason + Act. Think, then do, then evaluate, then decide what's next.
bulletPoints: A (10 min): ReAct pattern explained. LIVE on projector: react-agent-example.html animated trace вҖ” watch an agent reason step-by-step. NanoClaw uses this exact pattern.

B (8 min): System prompt anatomy. The magic isn't in the code вҖ” it's in the SYSTEM PROMPT. The agent loop is identical to Session 5's run_agent(). Exercise: write a 3-sentence ReAct prompt for a different domain.
C (10 min): Three Levels live demo in notebook. Same question, three approaches: no tools вҶ’ Session 5 agent вҶ’ ReAct agent. Same tools for Levels 1 & 2 вҖ” watch the escalation in real-time.

agenda.title: Part 3: Build a ReAct Research Agent
agenda.description: Hands-on in Colab. Build an agent that researches, evaluates sources, and synthesizes вҖ” with visible reasoning at every step.
bulletPoints: Phase 1 (10 min): Bring back your Session 5 tools вҖ” same real Wikipedia API, web fetcher, and calculator. Verify they still work. Then see the Three Levels: no tools vs Session 5 agent vs ReAct agent вҖ” same question, same tools, dramatically different behavior.

Phase 2 (10 min): Wire the ReAct loop вҖ” it's your Session 5 run_agent() with ONE change: the system prompt tells the agent to THINK before acting. Run the same question and watch the transformation.
Phase 3 (15 min): Push the agent вҖ” try different topics, explore failure modes with REAL APIs. Wikipedia 404s, vague questions, made-up companies. When does it struggle? When does it recover?
Phase 4 (5 min): Generate a structured research report from the agent's conversation вҖ” key facts, source quality, confidence level
Phase 5 (5 min): Build Your Own Tool challenge вҖ” add a 4th tool using a real API (weather, country data, etc.). See the worked example, then build your own.

agenda.title: Break
agenda.description: Stretch, grab coffee. Your agent now reasons before it acts вҖ” that's the production difference.

agenda.title: Part 5: Multi-Agent Systems & MCP
agenda.description: One agent is useful. A team of agents is powerful. And MCP is how they connect to the world.
bulletPoints: A (15 min): Three architectures вҖ” Pipeline (assembly line), Debate (adversarial review), Orchestrator (manager + workers). Org chart analogy: just like companies have departments.
Quick design exercise in pairs (4 min): Pick a business process, sketch a 3-agent pipeline on paper.
B (10 min): MCP = USB-C of AI. Build a tool once, every agent uses it. 1,000+ servers already exist.
NanoClaw's ipc-mcp.ts: See how a real production agent connects to tools via MCP.
Browse modelcontextprotocol.io live вҖ” Slack, GitHub, Google Drive, databases, and more.

agenda.title: Part 6: Business Automation & Agent ROI
agenda.description: Where do agents create real business value? A framework for deciding what to automate, augment, or leave to humans.
bulletPoints: The Automation Matrix: plot tasks on Volume (how often?) Г— Judgment (how much thinking?). Four quadrants: automate / augment / assist / don't bother.
Case studies: Invoice processing (full automation), sales outreach (agent + human review), strategy consulting (human + AI assist)
ROI formula: (Hours Saved Г— Hourly Cost Г— Error Reduction) вҲ’ Agent Cost = Monthly Value
The Trust Equation: Agent autonomy scales as stakes shrink. High stakes = human in the loop. Low stakes = let it run.
Quick discussion: What task from your job or internship would you automate first? Where on the matrix does it land?

agenda.title: Part 7: The Road Ahead + Wrap-Up
agenda.description: Where we've been, where we're going, and why the agentic arc is the future of this course.
bulletPoints: Journey so far: AI Fundamentals (S1-2) вҶ’ Prompts & Products (S3-4) вҶ’ Real-World Agents (S5) вҶ’ Agent Patterns (TODAY)
The agentic arc ahead: RAG (S7) вҶ’ Multi-Agent Build (S8) вҶ’ Industry Agents (S9) вҶ’ Guardrails (S10) вҶ’ Evaluation (S11) вҶ’ Final Project (S12-13)
Homework preview: Extend your agent, critique its research, design a multi-agent system, explore MCP
The code difference between your Session 5 agent and today's ReAct agent was ONE system prompt paragraph. But the THINKING difference was everything.

resources.title: Session 6 Lecture Guide
resources.description: Projectable lecture companion вҖ” visual slides for the full Session 6 lecture with key diagrams, code comparisons, and interactive navigation.

resources.title: Advanced Agent Patterns
resources.description: Visual explainer: ReAct reasoning, Chain-of-Thought planning, multi-agent architectures, and MCP. The patterns that power production AI agents.

resources.title: ReAct Agent: Live Walkthrough
resources.description: Watch a ReAct agent think step-by-step: search, evaluate sources, extract facts, and synthesize a research report. Interactive trace with real data.

resources.title: Research Agent Workshop Notebook
resources.description: Take your Session 5 tools, change one system prompt, and watch the behavior transform. Three-level comparison, failure exploration with real APIs, and a structured report generator.

resources.title: Anthropic Agent Documentation
resources.description: Official guide to building agentic systems with Claude. Covers tool use, multi-step reasoning, and best practices for production agents.

resources.title: Model Context Protocol (MCP)
resources.description: The open standard for connecting AI agents to tools and data sources. Think USB-C for AI. Browse 1,000+ available servers.

resources.title: Building Effective Agents (Anthropic)
resources.description: Anthropic's guide to building effective agents. Practical patterns, common pitfalls, and when to use agents vs. simpler solutions.

resources.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting
resources.description: The original research paper that introduced the ReAct pattern. Showed that interleaving reasoning and action dramatically improves agent performance.

resources.title: Claude Code
resources.description: Anthropic's agentic coding tool. A real-world example of everything we discussed: ReAct reasoning, tool use, multi-step planning, MCP.

activities.title: Build a ReAct Research Agent
activities.description: 45 minutes in Google Colab. Build an agent that uses ReAct reasoning to research, evaluate, and report:

steps.title: Three Levels of Intelligence (10 min)
steps.description: Bring back your Session 5 tools (real Wikipedia, web fetch, calculator). Run the same research question three ways: no tools, Session 5 agent, ReAct agent. See the difference one system prompt makes.

steps.title: Wire the ReAct Loop (10 min)
steps.description: The agent loop is your Session 5 run_agent() вҖ” the only change is the system prompt that tells the agent to THINK before each action. Watch the same tools produce dramatically better research.

steps.title: Push the Agent (15 min)
steps.description: Test failure modes with REAL APIs: Wikipedia 404s for made-up companies, vague opinion questions, ambiguous queries. Does the agent recover? Does it admit uncertainty? Does it hallucinate?

steps.title: Report + Build Your Own Tool (10 min)
steps.description: Generate a structured research briefing from the agent's work. Then add a 4th tool using a real API (weather, country data, etc.) вҖ” see the worked example and build your own.

activities.title: Multi-Agent Design Challenge
activities.description: Design a multi-agent system for a real business process. Think like an architect:

steps.title: Pick a Business Process
steps.description: Choose one: customer support ticket handling, weekly competitor report, content publishing pipeline, or employee onboarding. Or pick something from your own experience.

steps.title: Define Your Agents (3+ agents)
steps.description: Name each agent, define its specialty, list its tools, and describe when it hands off to the next agent. Think org chart вҖ” each agent is a department.

steps.title: Draw the Flow
steps.description: Sketch the pipeline: triggers, handoffs, decision points, and human review gates. Where does a human need to approve? Where can agents run autonomously?

steps.title: Present to Another Pair (3 min)
steps.description: Walk them through your design. They ask: Where could this fail? What needs human oversight? What's the ROI? How does information distort across agents?

activities.title: Automation ROI Assessment
activities.description: Apply the Automation Matrix to a real business process and calculate potential ROI:

steps.title: Pick a Process You Know
steps.description: Choose a repetitive business task from your job, internship, or a company you admire. Something done at least weekly.

steps.title: Map It on the Automation Matrix
steps.description: Plot it on two axes: Volume (how often?) and Judgment Required (how much human thinking?). Decide: Full automation, augmentation, or human-only?

steps.title: Calculate the ROI
steps.description: Estimate: Hours per week Г— hourly cost Г— 52 weeks = annual cost. Subtract agent cost (~$50-200/month API). Factor in error reduction. What's the payback period?

steps.title: Identify the Risks
steps.description: What could go wrong? What's the worst-case failure? Where do you need a human-in-the-loop checkpoint? What's the cost of a wrong answer?

takeaways.text: ReAct = Think before you act. The best agents don't just execute вҖ” they reason about what to do, observe results, and adapt. This pattern separates toy demos from production systems.
Agent teams beat solo agents. Just like human organizations, specialized agents collaborating on complex tasks produce better results than one agent trying to do everything.
MCP is the USB-C of AI. The Model Context Protocol means you build a tool integration once and every agent can use it. This standard is reshaping how agents connect to the world.
Automate the routine, augment the judgment. The Automation Matrix helps you decide what to fully automate, what to augment with AI, and what must stay human.

The code changed one paragraph. The thinking changed everything. Your Session 5 agent вҖ” same tools, same loop вҖ” became a research powerhouse with one system prompt change. That's the ReAct pattern.

homework.label: Extend:
homework.text: Add a 4th tool to your research agent (e.g., competitor_lookup, financial_data, or industry_news). Test it with 3 different business questions and document the reasoning traces.

homework.label: Analyze:
homework.text: Pick one research output from your agent. Write a 1-page critique: Were the sources credible? Did the reasoning have gaps? What would a human analyst do differently?

homework.label: Design:
homework.text: Write a 1-page multi-agent system proposal for a business process you care about. Include: 3+ agent roles, tools for each, handoff logic, human review gates, and estimated ROI.

homework.label: Explore:
homework.text: Browse modelcontextprotocol.io and find 3 MCP servers relevant to your final project idea. Write one paragraph each on how they would help your agent.

homework.label: Read:
homework.text: Read Anthropic's 'Building Effective Agents' guide. Write 3 key takeaways and identify one scenario where you should NOT use an agent.

nextSession.title: Session 7: Knowledge Agents вҖ” RAG
nextSession.description: Your agent is smart, but it doesn't know YOUR business. Add company knowledge with Retrieval-Augmented Generation.
